title: Module 2 quiz: Designing tools agents can actually use

questions.prompt: Your agent picks the wrong lookup tool 50% of the time. The model is current-generation Sonnet. First fix?

choices.text: Rewrite tool descriptions. Most wrong-tool bugs are description bugs — vague descriptions, identical-sounding names, missing triggers and anti-triggers. Tighten before swapping models.

questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the model's API doc. Distinct names + clear trigger phrases + 'when NOT to use this' + parameter examples routinely lifts selection from 50% to 95%+. Fix descriptions before reaching for a bigger model.

questions.prompt: Why set `additionalProperties: false` on tool input schemas?

choices.text: Stops the model from inventing fields you didn't define. Without it, models occasionally emit hallucinated fields that crash handlers or get silently ignored. With it + required + enums, the input shape is exact.

questions.explanation: Lock the schema. Pair with `required` for must-have fields and `enum` for fixed-value sets. Validate again at the runtime to catch edge cases.

questions.prompt: An agent's `issue_refund` call timed out and the agent retried. Two refunds went out. Right design fix?

choices.text: Tell the agent not to retry.
Require an `idempotency_key` on side-effect tools. Generate it once per task, pass it on every retry. Handler stores `(key → result)` for a few days; repeat calls return the stored result.

questions.explanation: Idempotency keys are the standard pattern for safe retry on side-effect operations. Handler dedups; repeat calls are no-ops with the same response.

questions.prompt: Which tools should always be behind an approval gate?

choices.text: Tools with irreversible side effects — sending messages, moving money, deleting data, changing external state irreversibly. Read-only tools and reversible internal actions don't need gates.

questions.explanation: Approval gates earn their friction on irreversible side effects. Layer with narrow tool design (so the gate isn't carrying the whole safety load) and a rule engine to auto-approve low-risk cases.

questions.prompt: What should a tool return on failure?

choices.text: Structured error: `{ error: 'NOT_FOUND', message: '...', suggested_action: '...' }` with `is_error: true`. Standardize codes (NOT_FOUND, RATE_LIMIT, CONFLICT, VALIDATION, etc.) across tools so the agent learns to recover.

questions.explanation: Structured, actionable errors let agents recover. Standardized error codes across tools form a vocabulary the model picks up — it learns what to retry, what to escalate, and what to tell the user.
